sections.summary: Taking aspirin together with warfarin markedly raises the risk of major bleeding. The combination is only used when a specific cardiovascular indication justifies it, under close supervision.
sections.mechanism: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase-1, impairing platelet aggregation for the life of the platelet, while warfarin suppresses vitamin K dependent clotting factors. Aspirin additionally damages the gastric mucosa, compounding gastrointestinal bleeding risk.
sections.clinical_effects: Higher rates of gastrointestinal and intracranial hemorrhage compared with either agent alone. Even low-dose aspirin contributes meaningfully to bleeding risk.
sections.management: Reserve combined use for clear indications such as a mechanical heart valve or recent coronary stent, at the lowest aspirin dose. Otherwise avoid concurrent aspirin and consider gastroprotection.
sections.monitoring: Monitor INR closely and watch for anemia, melena, or other bleeding signs. Reassess the ongoing need for dual therapy regularly.

evidence.citation: Dentali F, et al. Arch Intern Med. 2007;167(2):117-124
